turns.content: Imagine I'm writing a Rust function that needs to run some network logic without blocking the main thread, but my CI is complaining about missing traits. If I just write an async block here and call it from sync code, what exactly happens under the hood when the compiler sees this?

When you use an async function in Rust, the compiler automatically desugars that function into a trait object that implements std::future::Future. Specifically, it generates a struct that holds your closure and implements the Future trait so it can be polled by runtime like tokio or smol.
So if I want to avoid this automatic wrapping and control the lifecycle myself, what does the Future trait actually require me to implement?
You need to implement a poll method on your type that returns Poll::Ready or Poll::Pending. This is how async code gets suspended between operations without blocking execution immediately.
Okay, so I'm building my own Future struct. What does the poll function signature look like exactly?

The poll method takes a context parameter which is an Arc<AtomicBool> that tracks whether the future should be woken up, and returns either Poll::Ready(value) or Poll::Pending so the runtime knows when to resume.
Wait, you said context earlier but now it's an Arc<AtomicBool>? What about the Waker trait? Is that separate?
The context parameter is typically a Waker or similar mechanism. In practice, most runtimes pass a Context object that contains both the waker and other state needed for polling.
So I need to manually implement these three components then? Poll method, context handling, and waker registration?
Correct. You must define a struct with a poll method that accepts the Context or Waker, handle the state transitions, and ensure the runtime can wake you up when needed.
This sounds complicated for someone trying to fix a failing CI pipeline. Can I just use existing crates instead of rolling my own?

You can absolutely use tokio or smol with their async runtimes, but if you need custom behavior like manual polling control or specific state management, implementing a simple Future yourself gives you precise control over the lifecycle.
Let me test this. If I implement poll that returns Poll::Pending and then set some flag, will the runtime actually pause execution until I call it again?
Yes, exactly. When poll returns Poll::Pending, the runtime suspends the current thread and resumes only when you invoke poll again or when a waker is triggered.
I'm getting it now. But what if my pipeline fails because I forgot to implement the waker part? How does that show up in error messages?
The compiler will give you an error saying your type doesn't implement Future, or at runtime you'll see unexpected behavior if the waker isn't properly registered.
Ah! So the real issue was I thought async functions were just magic but they're actually just a wrapper around this manual implementation. That explains why my CI failed.
You've hit it. The compiler treats async fn as a Future trait implementation, so if your runtime can't poll correctly or the waker isn't set up right, everything breaks.
